questions.question: State and prove the basic pigeonhole principle.
keyPoints: Statement: If n items are placed into m containers with n > m, then at least one container holds more than one item
Proof by contradiction: Assume each container holds at most 1 item, then maximum total = m items, contradicting n > m
Therefore at least one container has ≥2 items

questions.question: State the generalized pigeonhole principle and prove it.
keyPoints: Statement: If n items are placed into m containers, then at least one container holds ⌈n/m⌉ items
Proof: If each container had at most ⌈n/m⌉ - 1 items, maximum would be m(⌈n/m⌉ - 1) < n
This contradicts distribution, so at least one has ⌈n/m⌉ items

questions.question: In a group of 27 people, prove that at least 2 have names beginning with the same letter.
keyPoints: Alphabet has 26 letters (containers)
27 people are the items to place
By pigeonhole: 27 > 26, so ⌈27/26⌉ = 2
Therefore at least 2 people share initial letter

questions.question: Prove that among any 10 integers, there exist two whose difference is divisible by 9.
keyPoints: Consider 10 integers and their remainders when divided by 9
There are only 9 possible remainders: 0,1,2,...,8 (pigeonholes)
With 10 integers, by pigeonhole, two have same remainder
Their difference is divisible by 9 (since difference of equal remainders = 0 mod 9)

questions.question: Show that in any group of 6 people, there are either 3 mutual acquaintances or 3 mutual strangers.
keyPoints: This is R(3,3) = 6 from Ramsey theory
Take any person A, they are related to other 5 (acquainted or stranger)
By pigeonhole, at least 3 of these 5 are of same type as A
If these 3 are all strangers to each other, done; if any two are acquainted, they form triangle with A

questions.question: How many cards must be drawn from a standard deck to guarantee 5 cards of the same suit? Explain.
keyPoints: There are 4 suits (pigeonholes)
Worst case: get 4 cards of each suit = 16 cards
17th card ensures 5th card of some suit
Answer: 17 cards

questions.question: Prove that if 20 numbers are selected from 1 to 50, there exist two whose sum is 51.
keyPoints: Create 25 pairs: (1,50), (2,49), ..., (25,26) - these sum to 51
These 25 pairs are the pigeonholes
Selecting 20 numbers might avoid completing a pair (one from each pair)
But with 26 numbers, we must complete a pair (26 > 25)
Wait - for 20, need to reconsider. Actually with 26 selections, guarantee sum 51
For 20: not guaranteed. Let's re-answer: Need 26 numbers to guarantee a pair summing to 51

questions.question: Prove that among any n+1 positive integers, there exist two whose difference is divisible by n.
keyPoints: Consider n+1 integers and their remainders when divided by n
There are n possible remainders: 0,1,2,...,n-1
By pigeonhole principle, two integers have same remainder
Their difference = (remainder - remainder) = 0 mod n
Therefore difference is divisible by n
This is a classic application of pigeonhole principle to number theory

questions.question: (a) Explain the birthday paradox showing why 23 people have >50% chance of sharing birthday. (b) How many people needed for >99% probability?
keyPoints: (a) Number of ways 23 people have all different birthdays: 365×364×...×343 / 365²³ ≈ 0.493
Probability of at least one match = 1 - (this) ≈ 0.507 > 50%
Counterintuitive because we compare pairs, not individuals: C(23,2) = 253 pairs
(b) For 99%, approximately 57 people needed
This demonstrates pigeonhole principle's probabilistic interpretation

questions.question: State and prove the Erdős–Szekeres theorem on monotonic subsequences.
keyPoints: Statement: Any sequence of (r-1)(s-1)+1 distinct numbers contains an increasing subsequence of length r OR decreasing subsequence of length s
Proof: Given sequence of length n = (r-1)(s-1)+1
For each element, define pair (a_i, d_i) where a_i = length of longest increasing subsequence ending at i
All a_i values are between 1 and r-1 (otherwise increasing length ≥ r)
If all d_i ≤ s-1, by pigeonhole, (r-1)(s-1)+1 values go into (r-1) boxes for a_i
This forces some two positions with equal a_i in increasing order
This creates contradiction, proving the theorem

questions.question: (a) Define Ramsey number R(m,n). (b) Prove that R(3,3) = 6.
keyPoints: (a) R(m,n) is smallest number R such that any graph with R vertices contains m-clique or n-independent set
(b) R(3,3) ≤ 6: Show K6 always has monochrome triangle (classic proof using pigeonhole on edges from one vertex)
R(3,3) > 5: Show K5 can be 2-colored without monochrome triangle (counterexample exists)
Therefore R(3,3) = 6
